one_line_takeaway: Mask-GCG adds a learnable token mask to existing GCG-family jailbreak attacks, empirically showing that ~17% of a fixed-length adversarial suffix is redundant and can be pruned to cut attack time ~16.8% and perplexity 24% while roughly preserving attack success rate.
key_finding: Pruning a minority of low-impact tokens preserves loss and attack success while cutting cost: for 30-token suffixes the average SCR is 7.5% across three models (max single-case compression 40%, i.e., 30 tokens down to 18), average attack-time reduction is 16.8% (16.5% at length 20, 17.0% at length 30), and median suffix perplexity drops 24% (4583.5 to 3486.4). ASR is maintained or slightly changed within reported 95% CIs, supporting the redundancy hypothesis that most (~83%) suffix tokens are essential but a small fraction are not.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Mask-GCG, a plug-and-play add-on to gradient-based jailbreak suffix attacks (GCG and its variants) that introduces a learnable, per-token soft mask over the adversarial suffix. The mask distinguishes high-impact tokens from redundant low-impact ones, increases the update probability of high-impact tokens while pruning low-impact ones, and thereby shrinks the discrete gradient/search space and lowers attack cost. The central scientific claim, framed as the title question ('Are all tokens in adversarial suffixes necessary?'), is that fixed-length GCG suffixes contain measurable token redundancy: the authors report that high-impact tokens account for roughly 83% of the suffix, so a minority can be pruned without loss of attack success. NOTE ON SCOPE: GCG, I-GCG, AmpleGCG, Probe-Sampling, Faster-GCG, and MAE are cited prior work, not contributions of this paper; Mask-GCG's own novelty is the learnable-mask + attention-guided-initialization + adaptive-pruning wrapper applied on top of those attacks.
threat_model: White-box (open-weights) jailbreak attack against safety-aligned LLMs. The adversary can compute gradients over the target model's tokens (needs model weights/embeddings), appends an optimized adversarial suffix to a harmful instruction, and aims to make the aligned model produce harmful content it would normally refuse. Mask-GCG is an efficiency/stealth improvement on this existing attacker capability rather than a new access assumption; the paper speculates (but does not demonstrate) that lower-perplexity suffixes may transfer better to black-box commercial models with perplexity filters. Attack success is measured by keyword/refusal-indicator detection on AdvBench harmful behaviors.
limitations: Evaluation is small-scale and white-box only: just 50 randomly sampled AdvBench instances, three open-weights models (Llama-2-7B/13B-Chat, Vicuna-7B), and keyword-based ASR (known to over/under-count vs. human or LLM judges). Compression gains are modest and highly variable (I-GCG SCR as low as 0.2-0.7% on Llama-2-7B because its fast convergence leaves few pruning windows), and some ASR numbers actually drop after masking (e.g., GCG Llama-2-7B length-20 ASR 58% vs Mask 68% but length-30 64% vs 62%; Llama-2-13B length-20 GCG 74% vs Mask 66%), which the authors attribute to edge-case over-pruning of minimal semantic triggers and argue is within the CI margin. The claimed black-box transfer benefit from lower perplexity is speculative and untested. The paper explicitly forgoes component ablations ('we prioritize the redundancy hypothesis over component tuning'), so the individual contributions of the learnable mask vs. attention-guided init vs. pruning are not isolated.

datasets_used: AdvBench harmful behaviors (50 randomly sampled instances)
quantitative_results: High-impact tokens exceed ~83% of the suffix in most cases
Average SCR for 30-token suffixes: 7.5% across three models (up to 10.5% single case; e.g., Llama-2-13b GCG+Mask 10.5%)
Max compression case: 30-token suffix compressed to 18 tokens (40% compression)
Average attack-time reduction: 16.5% (length 20), 17.0% (length 30); paper headlines 16.8% average
Average attack times: GCG 935.0s/1120.9s vs Mask-GCG 780.3s/930.5s (len 20/30)
ASR averages: GCG 77%/81% vs +Mask 77%/78%; I-GCG 100%/100% vs +Mask 99%/100%; AmpleGCG 97%/99% vs +Mask 99%/99% (len 20/30)
Median perplexity (GPT-2 evaluator, 600 suffixes): 4583.5 (GCG) to 3486.4 (Mask-GCG), a 24% reduction
Example 95% CI overlap: Llama-2-13b len20 GCG ASR [61.8, 86.2] vs Mask-GCG [52.9, 79.1]
